data.title: Simply Good Foods Doesn't Look Like A Growth Stock Anymore
data.description:
In the long term, Simply Good Foods (SMPL) might still have two growth brands. But this company’s Q2 2026 results were much worse than I expected. This company now has two struggling brands and one stable one. Its growth brands aren’t growing. And it also reported impairment losses on two of its brands. Simply Good Foods still has a strong balance sheet and enough cash flow to buy back more stock. But now, it may also need to invest in marketing to fix its brands. This stock doesn’t have a growth premium right now, but it doesn’t deserve one either.

I still think it’s better to look at the performance of each of this company’s brands individually. In Q2 2026, the company’s growth brand Quest posted 0.3% sales growth. OWYN, which previously experienced product quality issues, posted -16.8% growth. And Atkins, which is a mature and declining brand, reported -26.6% growth. As a result, Simply Good Foods reported -9.4% overall revenue growth and is now guiding for full-year revenue growth of -7% to -10%.

The company’s bottom-line results also got worse. Simply Good Foods also reported that its adjusted gross margin was 32.8%, down from 36.3% in Q2 2025. Additionally, the company reported a -$249 million impairment loss because of Atkins and OWYN, so it will report a GAAP loss in 2026. And its adjusted EBITDA fell 18.4% to $55.5 million.

But value investors might still be interested in Simply Good Foods. This company is trading below book value. If you don’t include the non-cash write-down, it’s still profitable, and it could still post a profit in 2027. But it might also be a value trap if its results continue to get worse. So, it’s worth considering why the company posted such weak results this quarter. The market for GLP-1 friendly foods is growing rapidly.

Simply Good Foods’ Target Market Is Growing Rapidly

In theory, Simply Good Foods should be well-positioned for a market where many consumers are taking GLP-1 drugs. You could consider all three of its brands to be GLP-1 friendly. Even though the company expects Atkins’ sales to fall, Atkins is still a health-food brand that could appeal to modern consumers. So, I think the expected growth of the GLP-1 food market would be relevant for Simply Good Foods.

The market research firm Future Market Insights provided statistics for the GLP-1 nutritional support market. According to the research firm, this market was worth $4.1 billion in 2025 and is expected to grow at a 12.2% CAGR for the next decade. Major companies in this market include Nestle (NSRGY) and Herbalife (HLF). This market includes companies that make products such as meal-replacement drinks, powders, and supplements, which is what Simply Good Foods makes.

Simply Good Foods May Have Lost Market Share

Large food companies, such as PepsiCo (PEP), also know that the GLP-1 nutritional support market is growing rapidly. And now PepsiCo is making protein Doritos. Doritos doesn’t really seem like a health-food brand to me, unlike Quest. But the Doritos brand is very strong, and PepsiCo has large economies of scale. And many consumers say Quest chips are expensive. So, it’s possible that Doritos undercut Quest on price and took part of its market.

An analyst asked Simply Good Foods CFO Christopher Bealer about this topic on the call. The CFO didn’t answer the question directly, but he did say that this information was included in the company’s outlook. And he also said that OWYN lost some distribution and that was also included in the company’s outlook. So, it’s possible that Quest and OWYN both lost market share to other companies. And large companies like PepsiCo and Nestle can also spend much more money to promote their GLP-1 products than Simply Good Foods can, so they may continue to gain market share.

Simply Good Foods’ Q3 2026 Guidance Is Very Weak

Simply Good Foods now expects -11% to -14% revenue growth in Q3 2026. And there’s a reason for this. In my previous article, I said there was a risk that the OWYN brand had been damaged by product quality issues. Now, Simply Good Foods is saying that the OWYN brand has been impaired and is writing off part of its value. So, it sounds like this might not be a short-term product quality issue and OWYN might also report weak results next quarter. And the thesis that growth from OWYN will help balance declining Atkins sales may no longer be correct. Simply Good Foods has to fix OWYN now.

Simply Good Foods Needs to Invest in Its Brands Now

This could be a tough year for functional-foods companies like Simply Good Foods. When your product is unique, your brand doesn’t need to be as strong. If other companies aren’t making protein chips, then the company that does make them gains customers because there are no alternatives. And if a company makes vegan meal-replacement drinks and other companies’ products are dairy-based, the vegan company also gains vegan customers because alternatives aren’t available. But now, large food companies are also making products aimed at rapidly growing markets like GLP-1 nutritional support. So, to defend its market, Simply Good Foods will have to invest in marketing to strengthen its brands.

Simply Good Foods doesn’t want to waste money on ads that don’t work, though. On the call, an analyst asked the CEO if the company is still planning to spend 10% of its revenue on sales. It might need to spend more than that because of the high-competition environment. CEO Joseph Scalzo said, “I think you should be thinking about 10% at this point as the ceiling until we prove that there's ROI justifying more. And then again, I believe that it's more about our choices than it is about competition.”

The Main Risk Is Private-Label Competition

Right now, private-label competition is not a major threat to Simply Good Foods. This company makes specialty food products, and its main competitors are other brand-name food manufacturers. But grocery stores and club stores could also make similar products now that they know this market exists. Recently, Celsius’ (CELH) stock fell after Costco (COST) started selling its own private-label energy drinks. Grocery stores could also make private-label protein chips and meal-replacement drinks. So, I think potential competition from private-label brands is a risk factor.

Updated Comparative Valuation for Simply Good Foods

Simply Good Foods is still trading at a large discount to its competitors. On April 11, this company had a forward non-GAAP P/E of 6.13. And again, it’s competing against Pepsi and Nestle, so I’ll compare it to those peers this time. I’ll also use non-GAAP P/E ratios because of Simply Good Foods’ recent write-offs. Pepsi currently has a forward non-GAAP P/E of 18.28. And Nestle has a forward non-GAAP P/E of 17.73. While Simply Good Foods is performing poorly, it’s also trading at a third of its peers’ multiples.

Updated Simply Good Foods Valuation

My original valuation for Simply Good Foods was way off because I wasn’t expecting the company to report such weak fiscal Q2 2026 results. And now, Simply Good Foods has also reported non-cash impairment losses for two of its brands. It looks like 2026 won’t be great for this company, but it could still perform better in 2027. So the best approach here might be to value it based on expectations for fiscal 2027.

Simply Good Foods’ updated fiscal 2026 guidance calls for revenue of $1.33 billion at the midpoint, which would be down 8.3% from 2025. The company also expects $221 million in adjusted EBITDA. That’s a 16.6% adjusted EBITDA margin. So, it sounds like Simply Good Foods is planning to raise its prices in the second half of 2026 to defend its margins, even if it loses some market share to competitors. And that makes sense. This company makes premium products for athletes and dieters, it’s not a discount brand. And higher whey prices also justify price increases.

If Simply Good Foods has lost some market share, its revenue could also be down in the first half of 2027, but it could stabilize after that. Demand for GLP-1 friendly food is still growing rapidly. So, if Simply Good Foods reported -8% revenue growth in H1 2027 and flat revenue growth in the second half of the year, its revenue would be down 4%. And its full-year 2027 revenue would be $1.28 billion.

The company’s cocoa costs will also be lower next year, which will strengthen its margins. And it also raised its prices. So, it expects its margins to be higher in 2027. Because of this factor, I’ll project that Simply Good Foods’ adjusted EBITDA margin will rise to 17% in 2027. With $1.28 billion in revenue, its 2027 EBITDA would be $218 million. If I subtract $30 million in depreciation and amortization, that’s $188 million in EBIT. With a 25% tax rate, Simply Good Foods’ fiscal 2027 net income would be $141 million.

Because of its recent buybacks, Simply Good Foods will have 92 million shares outstanding at the end of 2026. So, if it doesn’t buy back more shares in 2027, which I can’t assume it will do, its 2027 EPS would be $1.53. With a 2027 P/E of 6, my one-year target price would be $9.18 per share. That’s 12% lower than the current price of $10.44 per share.

Conclusion

It looks like Simply Good Foods might have used this quarter to get the bad news out of the way by providing very low guidance and reporting impairment losses for its brands. This could be a situation where the company is resetting expectations, because its new guidance could be easy to beat. And if its brand resets succeed, it could report stable revenue in the second half of 2027. Quest chips still have a lot of fans, so I don’t think this company is in permanent decline, although it does have a lot of work to do on its brands now. I also think its margins will be higher next year. But even with higher margins, it looks fairly valued because it’s now expected to post much weaker results. So, it’s still a hold.

data.title: Applied Digital: Post-Earnings Clarity Confirms An Accelerated Path To $1 Billion NOI Target
data.description:
Since last covering Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) in July last year, its stock is up 125% on the back of delivering the first 100 MW building under its CoreWeave (CRWV) lease on schedule and securing a second lease with Oracle (ORCL) at another campus. Recently, the company reported its FQ3 earnings that included its first full quarter of lease revenue. In my opinion, the company’s Q3 earnings showed the potential upside potential of its HPC hosting segment, which reported a 24.7% operating margin in its first full quarter.

Earnings aside, Applied Digital’s long-term prospects as an AI landlord significantly improved over the last week after amending its lease agreements with CoreWeave. These amendments effectively moved the tenant of the Polaris Forge 1 campus to an investment-grade rated CoreWeave special purpose vehicle (SPV) instead of the parent company, which holds a junk bond rating.

This is a significant development, in my opinion, as it allows the company to raise the final debt tranche related to the buildout of the third building at Polaris Forge 1 and refinance its existing high interest debt related to the campus at better terms, reducing its overall cost of capital. At the same time, the company secured parent level guarantees and a $50 million letter of credit to further de-risk its lease agreement with CoreWeave.

On another note, I expect Applied Digital to announce a new lease agreement for its newest campus, Delta Forge 1, by the end of this week. This is due to the covenants of its $100 million development loan facility with Macquarie allowing for the acceleration of the loan if the company doesn’t reach a final lease agreement with an investment grade hyperscaler for Delta Forge 1 by April 18th.

With Applied Digital’s management reiterating the $1 billion NOI target within 5 years in the Q3 earnings call, I’m forecasting the company to achieve this target in FY 2028 as Delta Forge 1 comes online and Oracle leases the remaining 100 MW available at Polaris Forge 2, which management expects to occur in the near term. In light of these factors, I’m reiterating my strong buy rating for Applied Digital with a $40 price target, implying 53% upside from current levels.

Q3 Overview

Applied Digital’s Q3 earnings showed a glimpse of the earnings upside potential of its HPC hosting segment. During the quarter, the company reported 139% YoY revenue growth from $52.9 million to $126.6 million, driven by realizing its first lease revenue, $71 million, from the 100 MW building at the Polaris Forge 1 campus in Ellendale, North Dakota.

The company’s HPC hosting revenue included $44.1 million in base rent, $18.9 million in non-recurring, low-margin tenant fill-out services, and $8.1 million in power pass-through arrangements and other ancillary revenue streams. Crucially, the segment generated an operating profit of $17.6 million at a 24.7% operating margin in its first full quarter, highlighting the segment’s earnings upside once the company’s remaining 500 MW of contracted leases come online.

Despite this, Applied Digital reported a GAAP net loss of $100.9 million compared to $36 million in the prior year as operating costs surged 514% YoY during the quarter from $22.7 million to $139.5 million. A primary contributor to this increase is the company reporting a one-time $59.7 million non-cash write down of its cloud services business’ assets to their carrying value. This charge was due to the segment no longer qualifying as held for sale, ahead of its anticipated reverse merger with EKSO Bionics Holdings (EKSO) by the end of FQ4.

Additionally, the company’s SG&A costs skyrocketed 251% YoY from $22.7 million to $79.7 million, mainly due to a $39.3 million increase in stock-based compensation related to a higher headcount and the accelerated vesting of stock awards.

In my opinion, Applied Digital’s GAAP results at this stage aren’t indicative of its long-term potential earnings performance due to the anticipated cloud services business spin-off and the PSUs related to bringing the initial buildings online. Therefore, I believe the company’s non-GAAP results, excluding the cloud services business’ results provide a better look into the company’s financial performance.

To that end, Applied Digital’s adjusted revenue during FQ3 came at $108.5 million, a 209% YoY increase from $35.2 million. This YoY growth drove a 1260% growth to operating income from $1.9 million at a 5.4% margin to $25.6 million at a 23.6% margin. Furthermore, adjusted EBITDA grew 605% YoY from $6.3 million to $44.1 million, representing a substantial margin expansion from 17.8% to 40.7%. More crucially, the company reported a non-GAAP income of $33.2 million at a 30.5% margin compared to a non-GAAP loss of $2.6 million in the prior year.

The company’s non-GAAP results highlight its earnings potential once SBC stabilizes after the initial wave of site buildouts. Currently, the company is actively building out 3 sites providing 900 MW of IT load and is marketing another 3 sites, suggesting that SBC could remain elevated through FY 2028. Despite the implied dilution risk, with SBC representing 27% of YTD FY 2026 revenues, I expect the rate of dilution to decline as the company brings more buildings online, generating additional lease revenue.

CoreWeave Lease Amendments

Last week, Applied Digital’s largest tenant, CoreWeave, announced a financing package that represents a paradigm shift in AI data center financing. CoreWeave closed an $8.5 billion delayed draw term loan facility that received an A3 rating by Moody’s and A (low) by DBRS, marking the first ever investment-grade rated financing secured by HPC infrastructure and a customer contract, $19 billion worth of contracts with Meta (META), as Bloomberg estimates.

CoreWeave’s latest financing represents a major boost to Applied Digital’s potential as an AI landlord, in my opinion, given that the loan facility is secured by the assets of an SPV, CoreWeave Compute Acquisition Co. VIII, LLC. Prior to securing this financing, CoreWeave and Applied Digital amended two of their three existing lease agreements, whereby the two 150 MW buildings leased by CoreWeave were moved to the SPV, thereby helping CoreWeave achieve an investment grade rating.

In exchange for helping facilitate CoreWeave’s financing package, Applied Digital received a parent level guarantee, meaning that the parent CoreWeave will backstop lease payments if the SPV fails to meet its obligations, and thus de-risking the lease agreement. Additionally, CoreWeave agreed to provide Applied Digital with a $50 million letter of credit and access to its audited financial statements and quarterly updates.

The letter of credit provides a significant buffer for Applied Digital as it can immediately draw that cash in case CoreWeave misses a payment without waiting for a court date. Meanwhile, the access to CoreWeave’s audited financials provides Applied Digital with better visibility into its largest tenant’s financial health. Yet the largest advantage for Applied Digital from these amendments is its ability to refinance its high interest $2.35 billion notes raised last November and raise the third and final debt tranche related to the third building at the Polaris Forge 1 campus at more favorable terms.

Back in November, the company issued 9.25% $2.35 billion secured notes to fund a portion of the construction of two buildings at its Polaris Forge 1 campus in Ellendale, North Dakota, which houses CoreWeave. The notes are due in 2030 and had an issue price of 97% of par, meaning that the company actually raised $2.28 billion. Accordingly, the effective interest rate of these notes is 10.15% to account for the amortized discount.

The primary contributor to the high interest on these notes is CoreWeave’s junk bond rating from the top three credit ratings. According to Bloomberg, CoreWeave issued two junk bonds last year that currently trade at 10% yield, whereas the average junk bond has a yield of 7.7%, highlighting CoreWeave’s high credit risk at the parent level.

With those 2 leases transferred to the investment-grade rated SPV, I expect Applied Digital to refinance the $2.35 billion notes and raise the final debt tranche for Polaris Forge 1 at comparable terms to its latest note offering. Last month, the company issued 6.75% $2.15 billion notes due 2031 at an issue price of 98% of par to fund the construction of 200 MW of IT load at its Polaris Forge 2 campus in Harwood, North Dakota. Accounting for the amortized discount, the effective interest rate of these notes comes at 7.30%, lower than the prior notes by around 2.86 percentage points.

The discrepancy between both note offerings’ terms is due to the tenant of the Polaris Forge 2 campus being Oracle. Last October, Oracle leased 200 MW of the 300 MW available at the campus last October, with ongoing talks to secure a second lease for the remaining 100 MW, per the October 2025 investor presentation. With that in mind, Oracle maintained an investment grade BBB credit rating with a stable outlook from both S&P and Fitch following its latest note offering in February, highlighting the steep difference between Oracle’s and CoreWeave’s credit health.

Now that the tenant of Polaris Forge 1 is CoreWeave’s investment grade SPV, I expect Applied Digital could refinance the $2.35 billion notes at 98% of par and 7% interest rate. Over a 5-year period, comparable to the original notes, this would lower the company’s annual interest expense by $52.9 million, while the lower discount to par would reduce the effective interest on the notes by 2.60 percentage points to 7.55%.

These savings would directly flow to Applied Digital’s bottom-line, effectively enhancing its profitability profile. At the same time, such a refinancing, along with the $2.15 billion note offering would represent a benchmark for a potentially another note offering for the construction of the company’s latest campus, Delta Forge 1.

Delta Forge 1 Lease Coming Soon?

In late January, Applied Digital broke ground on Delta Forge 1 located in a southern US state, which is speculated to be Louisiana. The facility will initially support 430 MW of utility power to enable 300 MW of IT load across two 150 MW buildings, with the ability to scale beyond 300 MW starting from 2028. Applied Digital expects initial operations at the campus to start in mid-2027 and is in active talks with another investment grade hyperscaler to lease the site, potentially bringing its tenants to 3 distinct hyperscalers to diversify its tenant base and reduce its customer concentration risk.

While a lease agreement for the campus hasn’t been reached yet, I expect an announcement could be made by April 18th. I believe this could be the case due to the covenants of Applied Digital’s development loan facility with Macquarie, announced last December. The development loan facility allows Applied Digital to draw up to $100 million for funding initial sourcing, planning, and construction costs associated with new data center projects.

The first draw under this facility, $45 million, was drawn on the closing date of the agreement, with an additional $55 million available for Applied Digital to draw from at any time. As of the end of Q3, the company has drawn a total of $87.1 million under this facility. According to the agreement, the loan has an 8% interest rate and matures at the earliest of December 18th, 2027 or July 18th, 2026, if Applied Digital hasn’t secured a lease agreement by April 18th for the site it drew the funds to develop.

Therefore, the company has to finalize the lease of Delta Forge 1 to avoid the acceleration of this loan and raise capital to fund a portion of the construction of the campus and pay back the high interest development loan to Macquarie. As is, the agreement stipulates that Applied Digital has to pay back the loan within 90 days after securing a lease agreement for the campus.

As for the economic profile of Delta Forge 1, I expect the potentially approaching lease agreement for the campus to be similar to Oracle’s lease at Polaris Forge 2 as the hyperscaler in discussions for the campus is investment-grade rated. On that note, Oracle’s investment grade credit rating has reflected on the pricing of its $5 billion lease as it implies a per MW rate of $25 million, whereas the $11 billion non-investment grade CoreWeave leases average a per MW rate of $27.5 million.

The difference in pricing suggests that CoreWeave is paying a premium to account for its non-investment grade credit rating due to its higher credit risk. On the other hand, Oracle was able to pay a rate lower than CoreWeave by 9.1% due to its superior credit profile that de-risks its lease agreement significantly. Based on this, I’m estimating a lease agreement for the 300 MW initially available at Delta Forge 1 to be worth $7.5 billion over 15 years.

With initial operations at Delta Forge 1 expected to start in mid-2027, I expect Applied Digital to start realizing lease revenue from this campus in FQ2 2028 and realize the full rent value from the campus in FQ4 2028 as it completes its buildout.

Applied Digital’s Key Differentiators

Beyond Delta Forge 1, Applied Digital is actively developing 3.87 GW of utility power across 8 additional sites where it has control of land or signed utility agreements for power, including expansions at current sites, according to its October 2025 investor presentation. Out of these sites, the company is actively marketing 3 sites, in addition to Delta Forge 1, located in North Dakota and two other unnamed states.

According to the Q3 earnings call, the company is in advanced discussions to lease these sites, with two of them under exclusivity with the same hyperscaler negotiating for the Delta Forge 1 campus. On that note, the company expects to secure agreements with this hyperscaler for Delta Forge 1 and the other two sites by the end of the year. In my opinion, this strong demand for Applied Digital’s data centers is driven by its strong execution amidst the significant headwinds facing US data center buildouts.

Nearly half of US data centers planned for this year are expected to be delayed or canceled due to a shortage of electrical equipment such as transformers, switchgear, and batteries, per Bloomberg. Currently, US manufacturing capacity for these equipment is unable to meet demand, and while imports from China, Canada, Mexico, and South Korea have increased, supply is still short.

Another headwind facing US data center buildouts is the limited grid power capacity that is unable to accommodate new data center demands. In Q4 2025, data center developers added 25 GW of electricity capacity to their pipelines, half of what was added in the prior quarter. Accordingly, analysts are estimating capex growth from the largest data center developers to decelerate this year for the first time since 2023, matching only 58% of last year’s growth.

A high profile example of these headwinds impacting an active US data center project is the breakup of the CoreWeave-Poolside 2 GW Project Horizon in West Texas. CoreWeave was set to be the anchor tenant of the first phase, 250 MW, of this project under a 15-year lease with an option for an additional 500 MW. However, the partnership reportedly fell apart after Poolside failed to stand up the first GPU cluster at the data center on time.

In contrast to Poolside's failure to deliver on schedule, Applied Digital delivered the first 100 MW building at Polaris Forge 1 to CoreWeave on schedule in CQ4 2025. The company also appears to be on the right track to deliver the second building at the campus on schedule in the second half of this year as it is currently commissioning medium-voltage switchgear at the building. As is, management reiterated in the Q3 earnings call that progress at Polaris Forge 1 and Polaris Forge 2 continues to be on schedule and on budget.

Applied Digital’s successful execution amidst the critical electrical equipment shortage is mainly due to its strategy of hoarding critical MEP equipment since 2023, when it started placing orders for switchgear transformers. The company has also bought out manufacturing capacity to supply future needs over 18 months ago, leading it to secure 600-700 MWs of MEP equipment per year.

At a time where compute capacity is constrained, CoreWeave terminating its Poolside partnership could be a tailwind for Applied Digital given its strong execution so far, especially with CoreWeave’s need for additional infrastructure to deliver on its growing backlog, which further expanded with two recent major deals.

On April 9th, CoreWeave and Meta expanded their relationship with a $21 billion deal for additional compute capacity through 2032. The next day, CoreWeave secured a multi-year deal with Anthropic to run Claude workloads on CoreWeave’s cloud at production scale.

Given the large scale of both deals, Applied Digital could be positioned favorably to secure additional lease agreements with CoreWeave at Polaris Forge 1. As is, CoreWeave already holds a first right of refusal for the campus’ entire 1 GW of expansion potential, and Applied Digital’s strong execution so far could be a key differentiator for expanding this relationship.

In addition to foreseeing the current equipment shortage, Applied Digital is actively attempting to address the grid bottlenecks by backing the independent power producer (IPP), Base Electron. Base Electron is an IPP formed by Applied Digital’s executives and directors with the purpose of developing power generation projects to add power to places on the grid that feed Applied Digital’s sites.

The first action by Base Electron was entering a $2.4 billion deal with Babcock & Wilcox (BW) for the design and build of four 300 MW natural gas-fired boilers and steam turbine generator systems to deliver 1.2 GW of power. The plant under this agreement is expected to begin operations in 2028, with an option for another 1.2 GW of generation capacity.

While Base Electron is obligated to make payments to B&W under this agreement, Applied Digital entered into a guarantee in favor of B&W in late February, guaranteeing the full and timely payments by Base Electron to B&W. In exchange for carrying this off-balance sheet obligation, Applied Digital received a 10% equity stake in Base Electron.

Although this arrangement means that Applied Digital is carrying about 50% of its nearly $4.8 billion debt, following the March note offering, as an off-balance sheet obligation, the guarantee can be terminated with the company maintaining its equity in case Base Electron IPOs, completes a $50 million financing, or through a termination fee of $50 million by August 1st, 2026 or $100 million thereafter.

These covenants significantly de-risk this arrangement, in my opinion, especially with management stating their expectations that Base Electron will eventually IPO in the Q3 earnings call. Meanwhile, the equity stake in Base Electron could have significant upside in the long-term if the 1.2 GW project proceeds successfully on schedule and on budget.

The Path To $1 Billion NOI Target

In the FQ2 earnings call, Applied Digital’s management stated a $1 billion NOI target to be achieved within 5 years. However, with the high likelihood of a potential Delta Forge 1 lease announcement in the short-term, I expect the company to achieve this target in FY 2028.

Polaris Forge 1

In FY 2028, the Polaris Forge 1 campus will be fully operational as the third 150 MW building is expected to come online in the first half of CY 2027, corresponding to the second half of Applied Digital’s FY 2027. Accordingly, the company will start realizing the full rent value of its $11 billion 15-year leases with CoreWeave, indicating an annual run rate of $733.3 million.

While the company expects this campus to generate an 88% NOI margin, I’m estimating its FY 2028 NOI margin at 85%, the lower end of management’s target, to reflect its potential early operations phase in the first half of FY 2028. As such, I’m estimating Polaris Forge 1 to generate an NOI of $623.3 million in FY 2028.

Polaris Forge 2

With Applied Digital in talks for leasing the remaining 100 MW at Polaris Forge, likely with Oracle since it holds a first right of refusal for the site’s full 800 MW expansion potential, a new agreement appears to be approaching as management anticipates the 100 MW to be contracted in the near-term, per the Q3 earnings call. With that in mind, the company expects to deliver the first 150 MW building at the campus in the second half of 2026, H1 FY 2027, and the second 150 MW building in the first half of 2027, H2 FY 2027.

Assuming similar pricing for the remaining 100 MW available at the campus, $25 million per MW over 15 years, the company could generate $500 million annually from Polaris Forge 2. Given the build out timeline of the campus, I’m forecasting Applied Digital to generate $250 million from the first building in FY 2028, reflecting a full year of operations, and $187.5 million from the second building, reflecting 3 quarters of operations, bringing my total FY 2028 revenue forecast for the campus to $437.5 million.

Although Applied Digital expects Polaris Forge 2 to generate an NOI margin of 86%, I’m forecasting its FY 2028 NOI at 75% to reflect its less mature status compared to Polaris Forge 1 and the early operations phase at the second 150 MW building. Based on this, I’m estimating Polaris Forge 2’s FY 2028 NOI at $328.1 million.

Delta Forge 1

As mentioned earlier, initial operations at Delta Forge 1 are expected to commence in mid-2027, suggesting that the first 150 MW building at the campus could be ready for service in FQ1 2028. Considering the company’s timeline history at Polaris Forge 1 and Polaris Forge 2, the second 150 MW building is likely to be ready for service in CQ2 2028, or FQ4 2028 for Applied Digital.

Since I’m forecasting Delta Forge 1’s lease agreement to be a 15-year $7.5 billion deal, I’m estimating each building to generate $250 million in annual revenue. In this way, I’m forecasting Delta Forge 1’s FY 2028 revenues at $187.5 million, reflecting 3 quarters worth of operations at the first building, to account for potential delays.

In terms of its NOI margin profile, I expect Delta Forge 1 to have a comparable profile to Polaris Forge 2 due to the investment-grade rated status of the potential tenant driving a lower per MW pricing. That said, I’m forecasting the campus’ FY 2028 NOI margin at 40% due to the initial operations phase at the first building and the costs associated with completing the build out of the second building diluting its margins. This brings my FY 2028 NOI forecast for the campus at $75 million.

Overall, I’m estimating Applied Digital’s HPC hosting business to generate $1.36 billion in FY 2028 revenue and $1 billion in NOI, representing a 75.6% NOI margin. Note that these estimates exclude any impact from the three additional sites the company is actively marketing, though such impact would be minimal in FY 2028.

With the company’s current REIT-like structure and stated future intention of converting into a REIT, I expect its EV/sales multiple to rerate near Equinix’s and Digital Realty’s average of 12.01 as it breaks ground on new sites and announces new lease agreements. By applying this multiple to my FY 2028 revenue target, my 2028 price target is $54.90.

On that note, I’m applying a 17% discount to this target to reflect potential risks such as construction delays, difficulties securing a Delta Forge 1 lease agreement prior to the April 18th deadline, and securing financing to reach a present value of $40.11, representing 53% upside potential from current levels.
